event: Barthelemi undergoing the ordeal of fire
symbols: fire — the ordeal of purification, God's judgment revealed through the elements
white-robed figures — the clergy or faithful witnessing divine vindication
tower in darkness — worldly authority observing the trial
description: A dramatic monochrome engraving showing a dense crowd gathered around a central area of bright flames and light. White-robed figures stand illuminated near the fire while a dark mass of onlookers fills the foreground and sides. A tall stone tower rises in the dark background at upper right. The scene is lit primarily by the central fire, creating strong contrasts between light and shadow. Smoke rises into a dark sky. The composition is atmospheric and heavily detailed with hundreds of small figures.
theological_description: The ordeal by fire was a medieval practice rooted in the belief that God would protect the innocent. While not a biblical narrative, it draws on scriptural precedents: the three young Hebrews — Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego — walked through Nebuchadnezzar's furnace unharmed because 'the God we serve is able to deliver us' (Daniel 3:17). Isaiah's promise echoes: 'When you walk through the fire, you will not be burned; the flames will not set you ablaze' (Isaiah 43:2). The medieval ordeal sought to make divine judgment visible, although the practice was eventually condemned as tempting God (Deuteronomy 6:16). The dense crowd watching represents the community's need to see justice rendered, reflecting the human desire for God to reveal right from wrong in unmistakable fashion.

scripture_refs.reason: The three youths surviving the fiery furnace — the prototype for the ordeal by fire
When you walk through fire you will not be burned — the faith behind ordeals of fire
Do not put the Lord your God to the test — the theological tension of trial by ordeal
